data.title: The 1 High-Yield Stock I'd Bet My Entire Income Portfolio On
data.description:
Introduction

Everyone loves dividends, both individual investors and massive corporations, as Bloomberg just reported that the Ping An Insurance Group boosted its stake in Chinese banks.

This news isn't groundbreaking or highly relevant to our research, but it serves as a good example of how billions of dollars ($23 billion to be exact) are chasing attractive valuations and over 4% in income, especially when Chinese government bonds yield around 1.7%.

Currently, we are not witnessing the same in the United States, as longer-dated government bonds yield more than 4.0%. Even ultra-short debt yields roughly 4.2%, which provides a great (risk-free) alternative to stocks that many older investors love after decades of benefiting from elevated capital gains.

If you think about it, for many older generations, things could not have gone better, as they are literally presented with a risk-free income opportunity right after they benefited from one of the biggest bull markets in U.S. history.

That's also one of the reasons why dividend stocks have underperformed the market recently (see below), as the incentive to buy 4-5% dividends hasn't been exciting for two major reasons:

  • Government debt yields 4-5%, which we just discussed.
  • Cyclical economic growth has been weak since 2021. When adding current geopolitical and new macroeconomic challenges, there is even more reason to opt for risk-free income, even if that income does not come with benefits like dividend growth and buybacks that many corporations offer.

Chinese investors don't have these options, which is why I believe China is the perfect example of what will (almost certainly) happen in the United States if rates come down, which is that billions (one could say trillions) of dollars suddenly need to find a new home.

Just like in China, American high-quality dividend stocks will become big winners after being overshadowed since 2023, when most investors preferred betting on high-flying growth stocks in areas like AI.

The aggressive purchases underscore a growing demand for high-yielding assets amid limited investment options. While Chinese authorities had guided state-backed insurers to allocate 30% of their new premiums to onshore listed shares annually, Hong Kong-traded bank shares offer more appeal with cheaper valuations and high dividend yields. - Bloomberg (emphasis added).

Since the end of 2023, Chinese banking stocks have added nearly 60%.

With that said, I don't mind that high-quality dividend stocks are unloved in the United States, the market where I have invested nearly 100% of my liquid net worth.

After all, it allows me to allocate a lot more money at attractive valuations, enjoying the long-term tailwinds that come with dividend investing, including consistently rising income, potentially market-beating returns, inflation protection, and reduced volatility.

This brings me to one tough question, which I get asked quite frequently.

What higher-income stock would I own if I could own just one?

Personally, I feel like putting this in a title borders on clickbait.

One might even argue that this border has been crossed already, as I have written similar articles, with titles like "Ultra High Conviction - If I Could Own Just 5 Dividend Stocks."

However, it still makes sense. While none of us will ever be put in a situation where we can own just one (or five) dividend stocks, there are benefits to asking these questions, including the fact that it triggers a thought process:

  • If you could buy or own just one stock in a certain segment or in general, what would it be? What kind of requirements do you have when looking for an investment? Are these requirements still valid? Do your requests still fit your current financial situation?
  • Do you already own that stock? If not, why is that? Would you sell one of your holdings to buy this new stock?
  • What makes for a good (in this case) high-income stock? Do your current investments meet these requirements?

So, while asking these "clickbait-y" questions may feel like a waste of time, I can honestly say that the thinking process I just outlined has helped me tremendously in consistently assessing my holdings.

That's why this article is about finding that one higher-income stock I would buy and own if I had to limit myself to just one company.

With that said because I know the company I am about to present to you later in this article is already wildly popular, I made sure to provide enough food for thought to make it worth your time, even if you disagree with me on the pick.

Being As Picky As Possible Provides Food For Thought

While this is, by no means, a golden rule, we can assume some of the best dividend stocks are part of the S&P 500, which is why I made the list below, which shows the 20 highest-yielding members of this index.

I turned this into an exercise, as I added a reason to each of them, explaining why I would not make it my only high-yield pick if I could buy just one. Please note that this does NOT mean that I do not like these stocks.

In fact, there are a few on this list that I am bullish on. This is purely a thought exercise that shows what I am looking for when trying to find the one stock I would own.

Name & Ticker

 Yield Reason It's Not Included
Dow Inc. (DOW) 10.1% Chemicals are too cyclical.
LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) 9.3% Just like Dow, it's too cyclical.
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) 7.1% I dislike the supply risks in biotech real estate.
Pfizer (PFE) 7.0% Long-term patent risks.
Healthpeak Properties (DOC) 7.0% Too many regulations in healthcare real estate for my taste.
Altria Group (MO) 7.0% Not enough societal benefits. The world wouldn't end if we all stopped smoking.
AES Corp. (AES) 6.7% I dislike the complexity and regulations tied to utilities.
Walgreens Boots (WBA) 6.6% Not relevant due to a takeover.
Conagra Brands (CAG) 6.5% Limited pricing power.
United Parcel Service (UPS) 6.5% Too cyclical and pressured by new entrants.
Verizon (VZ) 6.4% I'd rather not pick winners in this competitive space.

Edison International (EIX)

 6.4% I dislike the complexity and regulations tied to utilities. Also, wildfire risks.
Ford Motor (F) 6.3% Unless it's an ultra-luxury producer, I avoid automobiles.
Kraft Heinz (KHC) 6.2% Limited pricing power.
Best Buy (BBY) 5.6% Brick-and-mortar retail is tough.
BXP Inc. (BXP) 5.6% I am not bullish on the future of office buildings.
Amcor Plc (AMCR) 5.6% The space is too cyclical and competitive.
Eastman Chemical (EMN) 5.5% Again, chemicals are too cyclical.
Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) 5.5% Ultra-low barriers to entry and cyclical risks.

Again, this is a thought exercise. I like quite a few stocks from the list above. I only came up with a reason why I would not go all-in if I had to.

With that said, I presented just 19 stocks above, as my go-to stock is one of these 20. It's a company that fulfills all of these criteria:

  • Consistent dividend growth: A long history of increasing dividends, even (especially!) through tough times.
  • Stable, diverse cash flows: Predictable income from a varied base of customers or assets.
  • Sustainable payout ratio: A Healthy balance between dividends and earnings, which leaves room for growth.
  • Strong balance sheet: Low debt and robust financial health for resilience.
  • Resilient industry: The company should operate in a non-cyclical sector with consistent demand.
  • Competitive advantages: It should be a business with lasting strengths and qualities that make it stand out.

As I already mentioned, when it comes to buying just one stock, there is no room for error, zero. We cannot afford to take chances, especially when it comes to the income safety aspect. So, if we want to combine growth and income, we need to be even more careful.

Luckily, there's a company that has all of this.

Realty Income Corporation (O) - Boring, But Impossible To Ignore

Realty Income was missing from the table above. As it yields 5.6%, it would make it the 17th-highest-yielding S&P 500 member.

I also believe it is, by far, the most popular REIT on the market, as this single-tenant net lease REIT has hiked its dividend for 30 consecutive years. Moreover, this dividend is paid monthly, which is a nice addition for investors requiring steady cash flows, especially if it's the only holding in this fictional scenario.

Also, while Realty Income is not at all a fast-growing company, its dividend still has a 4.3% long-term CAGR, with a five-year CAGR of 3.6%, protecting investors against moderately elevated inflation for many decades. The payout ratio is roughly 75%, a very healthy number in the REIT space.

When it comes to inflation, rents are usually protected by rent escalators. However, often these are capped in the low-to-mid 3% range, which means elevated inflation won't be covered by these contracts.

Leases generally provide for limited increases in rent as a result of fixed increases, increases in the consumer price index, retail price index in the case of certain leases in the U.K. (typically subject to ceilings) or increases in clients’ sales volumes. We expect that inflation will cause these lease provisions to result in rent increases over time. During times when inflation is greater than increases in rent, as provided for in the leases, rent increases may not keep up with the rate of inflation and other costs. - O 2024 10-K (emphasis added).

However, the good news is that Realty Income is a net lease REIT that collects more than 90% of its rent from businesses operating in very resilient segments that are protected against economic downturns and e-commerce disruption.

I also believe that these relationships and assets protect the company against Artificial Intelligence, which may be one of the most disruptive forces in decades.

Because of its net lease structure, tenants pay for maintenance, taxes, and insurance, which massively reduces inflation risks for Realty Income.

This model has allowed The Monthly Dividend Company to deliver an average total operational return of 11% per year over its 30-year public history, which is based on the combination of adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) per share growth and a dividend yield that has averaged 6%, similar to its current level.

Even better, as we can see above, during this period, the company's annual returns have never fallen below 5%, which shows its resilience across various market cycles, including economic downturns, periods of high inflation, and various capital market conditions, including both loose and tight financial conditions.

On top of that, the company has consistently reported high occupancy rates, and low bad debt expenses.

  • On average, 99.6% of its portfolio has been unaffected by credit losses.
  • Since 2014, the portfolio has realized just 0.4% of bad debt expenses.
  • Thanks to its producer tenant portfolio, the company has a great track record when it comes to re-leasing, dispositions, and adding value to assets.
  • During recessions, its focus on "mission-critical" real estate has provided consistently above-average occupancy ratios, with Q1 '25 seeing a 98.2% occupancy rate.

In order to grow, the company is using a number of tailwinds:

  • Sector diversification: The company is not limited to retail. In recent years, it has executed aggressive investments in industrial properties, data centers, and gaming assets across two continents. This includes investments in the United Kingdom and the Bellagio in Las Vegas. It owns roughly a fifth of this Las Vegas landmark. This increases diversification and allows it to use secular growth in other areas.

  • Geographic expansion: As I already briefly mentioned, Realty Income has expanded beyond the United States, including the U.K. and six other Western European nations. For example, in 2019, the company entered the U.K. with a $0.5 billion sale-leaseback deal with Sainsbury's. This company is the U.K.'s second-largest grocer. Currently, Realty Income has more than $10 billion in the United Kingdom.

Note that sale-leaseback deals are transactions where a company sells its buildings to a landlord. Instead of using a bank or bond market for financing, a company simply uses its building. This is a huge market, especially in light of elevated interest rates.

Because of its A-range credit rating, Realty Income is in a great spot to engage in these transactions. And, last but not least, Europe has less competition in the sale-leaseback market, with access to lower rates for Realty Income, as it can tap into Europe's capital markets.

  • A versatile capital structure: Beyond traditional sale-leaseback deals, Realty Income engages in development funding and credit investments for select clients with strong long-term prospects. Essentially, this approach allows the company to tailor solutions to client needs while improving revenue streams.

On top of that, advanced technological tools allow the company to apply a "data-driven" approach. This makes sense, as the company owns more than 15,600 properties. That's a LOT of data.

Thanks to this approach, the company noted its recent presentation at the Nareit REIT week to report re-leasing spreads of more than 105% with minimal capital investment, which also reflects strong demand for its properties.

In fact, while some may bring up that Realty Income's size is a disadvantage, it has recently grown much faster than in the 1996-2012 period, when it was a much smaller company, which is expected to last, as we can see below.

With that said, the valuation reflects the current interest-rate environment, as Realty Income trades at 13.7x AFFO. This is way below its multi-decade average of 17.2x.

A big reason why this company is cheaper is the surge in longer-term interest rates, which compete with the yield on REITs.

However, even in this environment, Realty Income is cheap, as analysts expect 2-3% annual per-share AFFO growth, a number I expect to slowly move to 3-4% if inflation remains sticky. When adding its 5.6% yield, we get an annual total return outlook of up to 10.0% without any valuation tailwinds.

While Realty Income does not have the growth profile of smaller REITs like Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), it has a higher yield, a lot of safety, and everything I am looking for in a long-term income investment.

Especially in a scenario of falling rates (like in China), I expect huge capital inflows into Realty Income and other high-quality dividend stocks.

That said, I don't own the stock, which is solely based on my decision to invest in dividend growth for at least a few more years. Once I shift my focus to income, I will be a big buyer of Realty Income stock.

Takeaway

Dividend stocks may be out of favor in the U.S. right now, but that's exactly what makes them so attractive.

With risk-free Treasuries offering 4-5%, it's no surprise income investors aren't rushing into these equities.

When rates eventually fall, the floodgates could open, just as we've seen in China, where high-yield bank stocks have surged due to aggressive buying from asset managers.

For me, the real question isn't if but when this shift happens. That's why it's important to use this environment to decide what to buy.

As we discussed in this article, if I had to pick just one high-income stock today, it would be Realty Income. It's boring, predictable, and built to last, with a 5.6% yield, 30 years of consecutive annual dividend growth, and a fortress-like portfolio of mostly recession-resistant properties.

While this is a totally fake scenario and other companies could have higher total returns, Realty Income checks every box, which is why it's at the very top of my to-do list when I decide it's time to squeeze more income from my portfolio.

Risks To My Thesis

Although Realty Income is a beacon of safety, a prolonged recession could hurt its tenants/occupancy ratio. Also, mismanagement of its balance sheet and failed multi-billion-dollar investments could hurt its growth outlook.

However, these risks are subdued.

data.title: Vale: Why I Keep Betting Against The Crowd
data.description:
VALE Stock: Previous Thesis And New Developments

I last analyzed Vale S.A. (VALE) on April 27, 2025 and rated the stock as a sell. More specifically, that article was entitled “Rio Tinto Vs. Vale: Inventory Doesn't Lie”. In the article, I explained the headwinds facing the mining sectors through the lens of their elevated inventories. Quote:

Since my last analysis, new tariffs announced by President Trump have added additional – and large – uncertainties to mining stocks. I expect high tariffs to disrupt global trade flows, decrease demand for basic metals, and increase operational costs for both RIO and VALE. As a reflection of these macroscopic pressures, the latest data show that both companies are experiencing elevated inventory. The inventory build up is even more concerning in the case of VALE.

Since then, there have been some new issues evolving around VALE stock. In this follow-up article, I will focus on the top 3 on my list: the changes in the prevailing sentiment, the developments of its divestitures, and also the potential of IPO of its nickel and copper units. After examining these developments, I consider the stock as a bull trap for reasons to be detailed in the rest of the article and thus maintain my sell rating.

Let me start with the sentiment change. As you can see from the following two figures, since my last analysis, VALE’s performance has been miserable either in absolute terms (with a negative total return of about 3%) or relative terms (e.g., when compared to the gain of the SP500). Against such a performance, the sentiment surrounding the stock has become extremely positive in the meantime, as you can easily tell from both the ratings from Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts. After examining their writings, I can see several common arguments underpinning their bullish theses, with the top two being the cheap valuation and also the prospects of a recovery in the demand for raw materials. Next, I will explain why disagree with both considerations.

VALE Stock: Valuation And Growth Outlook

The signal digit P/E ratio is certainly attractive, as you can easily tell from its valuation grade shown below. VALE's valuation, in terms of various P/E ratios, consistently appears undervalued compared to its sector median. For instance, its P/E on a Non-GAAP and TTM basis is only 7x, significantly lower than the sector median of 16.29 by more than -57%.

However, potential investors should be aware of two caveats. First, its current P/E ratios are at a premium when compared to its historical averages as seen. And second, its current P/E ratios are not cheap at all when adjusted for growth potential either. As you can see from the next chart below, VALE's EPS is projected to experience uneven growth in the next few years. In FY 2025, the EPS estimate is $1.78 with a year-over-year decline of -2.18%. The EPS is expected to slightly increase to $1.87 in FY 2026 with a 5.42% YoY growth. All told, its EPS is expected to decline from $1.78 in 2025 to $1.62 in 2029, translating into an overall negative growth rate of -1.9% CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) in the next five years.

I can see good reasons for such a secular decline of its EPS. The leading factor in my model is the uncertain demand of raw materials in the coming years. First and foremost, I am very concerned about the real estate and infrastructure demand in China, a sector that is still struggling despite the country’s recent stimulus efforts. The real estate and infrastructure sector are among the largest consumers of raw materials in the country and China is a leading customer for VALE’s ores in recent years. Second, I am also worried that the ongoing trade conflicts could further dampen the global economy's growth prospects and the demand for raw materials.

When the above gloomy growth outlook is factored in, VALE stock’s single-digit P/E is not that attractive anymore. Given the stock’s status as a mature mining company, I believe the following Graham’s P/E is very applicable:

The formula as described by Graham originally in the 1962 edition of Security Analysis is as follows: P/E = 8.5 + 2*g, where g is a reasonably expected growth rate for the next few years.

With a negative 1.9% growth rate according to the market consensus, the Graham P/E for VALE would be around 4.7x only (i.e., 8.5 - 2 * 1.9). At the price of this writing, its FY1 P/E is about 5.33x as seen above, translating into a valuation premium rather than a discount.

VALE Stock: Financial Strength Is Improving

Another factor of concern is VALE’s use of leverage. As you can see from the following chart, VALE relies much more heavily on debt financing when compared to its peers, which to a large degree justifies its lower P/E when compared to peers as seen in the valuation grade chart. For instance, VALE's financial debt to equity ratio current hovers around 0.406, about threefold of Rio Tinto PLC (RIO)’s ratio of 0.127 and/or BHP Group Ltd (BHP)’s ratio of 0.158.

However, on the positive side, the company has been improving its financial strength lately. When the March interim concluded, cash on hand was almost $4 billion. In addition, Vale’s pending sale of 70% of its stake in Alianca Geracao, which operates in the Brazilian energy market, could bring in an additional $1 billion of cash based on my estimates. Moreover, the company is also considering IPO of some of its units, as elaborated on next.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

According to the following news release, VALE could IPO its nickel and copper units in the near future. The above IPO, if successfully materializes and when combined with the divestiture of its Alianca Geracao assets, could substantially strengthen its balance and provide more capital allocation choices for the company.

Seeking Alpha News: The new CEO of Vale's base metals unit, Shaun Usmar, said he aims to prepare the nickel and copper business for a potential IPO by 2027, if not sooner, Bloomberg reported Tuesday. Separating Vale Base Metals from its parent company as a publicly traded entity could allow it to command a premium over other metals producers, Usmar said in an interview, and selling shares in the base metals division would help finance Vale's (VALE) ambitious plan to spend as much as $30B to boost production capacity across its nickel and copper mines in Canada, Brazil and Indonesia.

All told, this follow-up article on VALE was mainly motivated by 3 new catalysts since my last writing: the changes in the prevailing sentiment, the potential of its Alianca Geracao divestiture, and the potential IPO of its nickel and copper units. After examining these developments, I keep seeing an unfavorable return/risk curve and keep disagreeing with bulls. Some of these developments can indeed help improve its financial strength and better position it for growth CAPEX investments. However, I see the uncertain secular demand as the dominant force under current conditions. Finally, I don’t consider the P/E attractive at all in the framework developed by Graham. These considerations have led me to reiterate my sell rating.

data.title: 2 'Sleep Well At Night' REITs For Retirees Well Positioned To Deliver Growth
data.description:
A few days ago, I began a series called “Put REITs In Retirement: Part 1” in which I provided a list of my first two (out of ten) REITs for Retirees. As I explained

“…by owning shares in REITs, the investor gets access to diverse portfolios of real estate that own, finance, and operate structures that are essential to neighborhoods, enable the digital economy, and build infrastructure.”

My objective with this portfolio of ten REITs is to select some of the best companies to own today based on their quality and value.

Recognizing that most retirees depend on income, I wanted to avoid speculative and/or highly leveraged companies that have a high risk of a dividend cut.

The first two picks (from the previous article) are Realty Income (O) and EastGroup (EGP) – both selected due to their strong fundamentals and attractive valuation.

As usual, I will focus on selecting these ten constituents based on their ability to generate sustainable and growing earnings, as well as safe dividends.

I believe that now it’s a great time to be purchasing REITs as the setup today is similar to the 70s and 80s when cap rates rose sharply and inflation spiked.

While Mr. Market is fearful that REITs can’t grow earnings (I’ll prove him wrong), the reality is that net operating income continues to grow, driven by scarcity (slowdown in construction) and rising replacement costs. A few days ago, I posted this chart on X (formerly Twitter):

I wrote that “REITs returned an average of 19.5% per year from 1975-1988, while the 10-year averaged 9.7% and inflation averaged 6.7%.”

Given the opportunity set that I see today, I see logic in Mark Twain’s famous words, “history doesn’t repeat itself, it rhymes.”

Just take a look at the chart below, and you can see that REIT earnings (funds from operations, or FFO per share) continue to increase, which is a good sign…

In addition, REIT dividends continue to increase…

So, given both the historical precedence that I just referenced as well as the current cycle we’re in now, REITs are well positioned to continue to deliver growth, amid the “higher-for-longer” rate environment and elevated inflation.

I would argue that REIT managers today are more experienced as they continue to make strategic investments in technology through prop-tech and/or AI that allows their companies to reduce costs and enhance portfolio value.

As you know, efficiency drives margins, and this is often overlooked when you consider the evolution of REITs over the least 60+ years.

So, without further ado, let’s examine the next two picks...

What's Up DOC?

Healthpeak Properties, Inc. (DOC) is a leading healthcare REIT that owns a portfolio of life science properties (35^ of revenue), medical office buildings (49% of revenue), and senior housing properties (11% of revenue).

Collectively, the 49 million square foot portfolio (of 700 properties) is worth an estimated $24 billion, with a $12.0 billion market cap, which makes the company the 4th largest healthcare REIT within our coverage spectrum.

As you can see below, Healthpeak has scale with nationwide coverage in markets such as San Francisco (23%), Boston (10%), Dallas (8%), San Diego (5%), Houston (4%), Tampa (4%), and many other markets.

These consist of health systems (33%), physicians (14%), large cap bio (11%), med device/R&D/University systems (9%), CCRH/SH *8%), Mida Cap Bio (7%), Private Biopharma (6%), Small Cap (6%) and other (4%). Here’s a snapshot of the Top 20 customers:

  • HCA Healthcare Health System 9%
  • CommonSpirit Health System 3%
  • McKesson Corporation Health System 1%
  • Ascension Health System 1%
  • University of Louisville Health System 1%
  • Northside Hospital Health System 1%.

Healthpeak also has a strong balance sheet, highlighted by 5.2x net debt to EBITDA (as of Q1-25) and $2.8 of liquidity. The company has strong credit ratings of BBB+/Baa1 2ith a debt ratio of 38%, as shown below:

Healthpeak has demonstrated solid growth in the outpatient medical (same-store growth of 5% in Q1) and CCRC (same-store growth of 15.9% in Q1) business, and the share buybacks.

In addition, the company gained occupancy within the life science sector (same-store growth of 7.7% in Q1), but there was a notable benefit from free rent burn off in the Q1.

One of the primary reasons for the selloff is related to lab leasing and bad debt expectations in a slowing economy, as well as the current VC funding environment. In addition, the potential impact from government funding is also an overhang worth watching.

Nonetheless, the latest Q1 results were solid, the company reported FFO per share of $.46 and AFFO per share of $.43, and same store growth of 7%. The company executed nearly one million square feet of leases, including 265,000 square feet of new leasing. The company maintained FFO as adjusted guidance in the range of $1.81 per share to $1.87 per share.

As seen below (using FAST Graphs), Healthpeak’s payout ratio is 74% which makes the dividend relatively safe. In addition, the consensus growth estimates for 2025, 2026, and 2027 are as follows: 3%, 3%, and 6% respectively.

Now let’s examine Healthpeak’s valuation compared with these peers:

As you can see, the company is trading at just 9x P/FFO, with a dividend yield of 7%.

Our most conservative forecast has DOC returning over 20% in the next 12 months.

This Apple Does Not Fall Far From The Tree

Apple Hospitality REIT, Inc. (APLE) is a lodging REIT that owns one of the largest and most diverse portfolios of upscale, rooms-focused hotels in the U.S. This portfolio of 220 hotels (and 29,767 guest rooms) is located in 37 states (and 85 markets).

This broad geographic diversification provides exposure to a wide variety of demand generators with a mix of business and leisure demand. The portfolio benefits from both large corporate negotiated and small and mid-market local negotiated business demand with low dependence on inbound international travel.

Apple invests in room-focused hotels with industry-leading brands that have broad consumer appeal. Specifically, Apple invests in select-service hotels that are franchised with industry-leading brands with proven appeal (as shown below).

Apple maintains a strong balance sheet. At the end of Q1 '25, the company had approximately $1.5 billion of total outstanding debt, approximately 3.3x the trailing 12 months EBITDA, with a weighted average interest rate of 4.8%.

In addition, Apple had cash on hand of approximately $15 million, availability under the revolving credit facility of approximately $500 million, and approximately 72% of total debt outstanding was fixed or hedged.

Low leverage has always been a key component of Apple’s strategy, providing stability and optionality across economic cycles.

Also, Apple reauthorized and extended its ATM Program in Q1 2024, providing availability for the issuance of common shares up to $500 million.

During Q1 '25, Apple’s comparable hotel total revenue was $324 million, down 0.4% vs Q1-24, and comparable hotels adjusted hotel EBITDA was $105 million, down approximately 5% from Q1-24.

First quarter comparable hotels RevPAR was $111, down 0.5%. ADR was $157, up 1%. And occupancy was 71%, down 1.5% as compared to the first quarter of 2024.

As viewed below, Apple’s room-focused operating model produces the strongest margins

At the end of Q1, Apple updated its outlook for 2025 and provided full year expectations that include (1) net income to be between $167 million and $195 million, (2) comparable hotels RevPAR change to be between negative 1% and 1%, (3) comparable hotels adjusted hotel EBITDA margin to be between 33.7% and 34.7% and (4) adjusted EBITDAre to be between $433 million and $457 million.

As viewed below, Apple has a safe 76% payout ratio (using AFFO per share) with growth expectations (using consensus data) of -5% in 2026, +3% in 2026, and +3% in 2027.

Shares are trading at $11.83 with a P/AFFO multiple of 9.2x (normal value is 12.1x). The dividend yield is 8.11%. As shown below, we forecast Apple could return 15% or more over the next 12 months.

In Conclusion

I hope you’re enjoying our Put REITs in Retirement series.

As I pointed out in the introduction section,

“given both the historical precedent as well as the current cycle we’re in now, REITs are well positioned to continue to deliver growth, amid the 'higher-for-longer' rate environment and elevated inflation."

I think it’s extremely prudent for readers, especially retirees, to own shares in REITs, amid the current rate environment. History has proven that REITs can perform well (based on fundamentals) in just about any market environment.

I’m still in the camp that rate cuts will drive REIT returns, as they always do; however, the argument to stay on the sidelines is without merit, as the brick-and-mortar asset class can benefit investors seeking income and capital appreciation.

As always, thank you for reading and commenting.

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Coty: Why I'm Watching This $4 Stock Now
data.description:
Coty Inc. (COTY) is a leading maker of perfumes that has been in business for over 100 years. It makes many perfumes under brands that most of us have heard of and used. This includes names like Calvin Klein, Gucci, Burberry, Hugo Boss and many more. I have watched Coty shares from time to time over many years, and after seeing the stock plunge, I wanted to take a closer look to see if it was a bargain worth buying now. With this in mind, let's take a closer look:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, Coty shares were trading for about $10, last August. It now trades for about $4.50 per share, which is below the 50-day moving average of $4.92 and way below the 200-day moving average, which is $6.56. The downtrend for this stock has been very strong for many months now, and based on this and some other fundamental factors (which I discuss below), it seems like the downtrend could continue in the months ahead. But, I would add that this stock has been so badly beaten-down that any good news could spark a major rally, but that is just not enough to make this worth investing in for me.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The earnings estimate (provided by Seeking Alpha) shown below, suggest this company could earn about $0.37 per share in 2025, and potentially a bit more in 2026 and 2027. Sales are not expected to show much growth in the coming years. With the challenges this company is facing, I would not be surprised if these earnings estimates are revised downward, and if so, the stock could continue to go lower. Coty has recently missed on earnings and lowered guidance, and it seems this could continue in upcoming quarters.

The balance sheet concerns me because this company has about $4.05 billion in debt and just around $243.5 million in cash. This amount of debt is too high in my opinion and it especially could be of concern if consumer spending weakens. Partially offsetting the debt load is the fact that Coty owns a stake in Wella, which it plans to sell this year. The proceeds from this sale could reduce the debt.

Potential Risk Factors

Some of the major concerns and potential risk factors for this company include weak sales in China, which has been impacted by Liberation Day tariffs. Another potential problem for Coty due to tariffs is that it imports products into the U.S. from other countries. It is working to mitigate these concerns, but it looks like weak consumer spending in China could continue for a while, and it could also take time to mitigate the increased cost of tariffs.

It appears that some potentially major strategic mistakes have been made by Coty. For example, in 2021, it acquired a stake in Kim Kardashian's beauty business for a whopping $200 million. But, earlier this year, the company sold that stake and declined to state the financial terms or details. This presumably suggests it was sold at a major loss, and it appears to have been a big strategic mistake to buy into the Kardashian's business in 2021, only to sell it a few years later.

The U.S. economy appears to be slowing down, and this could lead to deteriorating sales for Coty in the coming quarters. As I wrote in this recent article, the impact from tariffs remains to be seen and there are some potential warning signs that the labor market is cooling. I would not be surprised to see another growth scare, if not a recession in the coming months. I also believe that foreign exchange fluctuations could be negatively impacting Coty, especially since the U.S. Dollar has dropped in value by nearly 10% over the last few months.

Coty is potentially planning to sell some assets it holds and possibly some brands or divisions. If these sales do not go as planned, it could negatively impact the stock price and the plans to improve the balance sheet. The balance sheet definitely seems to be of concern for investors, and this can be seen by looking at the Altman Z Score for Coty, which is 0.60. The Altman Z Score is used to rate bankruptcy risks and the closer the score is to zero, the higher the potential risk in terms of solvency. Seeking Alpha provides Altman Z scores for stocks, which can be found under the "risk" section on the summary page for each stock. Just for the sake of comparison, the Altman Z Score for Apple (AAPL) is nearly 10, because it has an incredibly strong balance sheet.

There Could Be Some Good News Coming

Coty owns a significant stake in Wella, and it has been selling off part of this stake over the past few years. It still retains a stake that represents more than 20% of Wella, and it plans to sell this remaining stake (which could be valued at around $1 billion), by the end of 2025. It is good to see that management wants to sell assets in order to pay down the debt load and a final deal to sell the remaining stake in Wella could boost the stock and the balance sheet.

Coty is also rumored to be considering the sale of some portions of its businesses or certain brands, and this could also generate cash for the company to pay down debt. Additionally, Coty is working to cut expenses, which could mitigate tariff expenses and other rising costs.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I believe there could be at least a couple more disappointing quarters for this company, and I think this could come from weak consumer spending in China and the U.S., and it could also see margin pressures from tariffs and currency fluctuations. However, with the shares now just in the $4 range, the stock market might be willing to look past these issues, and instead focus on potential asset sales and what could be a brighter future. If everything goes according to the plans management has laid out, this stock could already be at rock-bottom levels and worth buying now. By waiting to buy, I could miss a big move in the stock if some good news comes out.

My Strategy For Coty Shares

I have not seen any insider buying recently and with the plunge, this stock has seen, I would like to see some insider buying. If I saw significant insider buying, it would make me more confident that this stock and this business has better days coming. If I do see insider buying, I would quickly change my wait and see strategy and hold rating to a buy rating. I plan to see how the next couple of quarters turn out as I believe there could still be some weakness ahead. I also think that with the shares trading near 52-week lows, there could be significant tax-loss selling pressure later this year. Because of this, I plan to take another look at this stock later this year and possibly start accumulating shares in Q4 in order to take advantage of potential tax-loss selling, and also to clear a couple more quarters of possibly challenging financial results.

In Summary

I think the risk to reward ratio for this stock is fairly balanced right now, which is why I would give it a hold rating. It could be poised to see more selling pressure for the remainder of this year as I believe consumer spending will be weak, and tax-loss selling could also put pressure on this stock. If I see some insider buying, or improvements in the balance sheet, I would consider buying, but for now, I am going to keep watching this stock. Please let me know what you think in the comments below.

No guarantees or representations are made. Hawkinvest is not a registered investment advisor and does not provide specific investment advice. The information is for informational purposes only. You should always consult a financial advisor.
